Document [31] (Title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Introduction) Herpes simplex keratitis is a common and potentially blinding condition caused by recurrent corneal infections with the herpes simplex virus (HSV). Herpes simplex keratitis remains the leading infectious cause of corneal ulcers and blindness worldwide. [1] [2] The most ubiquitous types of HSV are HSV-1 and HSV-2, which affect humans, their only natural hosts. HSV-1 is usually the cause of infections in the oral, labial, and ocular areas; HSV-2 primarily causes lesions in the genital region, although this epidemiology is now changing. Primary infection with HSV occurs after direct contact inoculation of mucosal or skin surface, often subclinical and going unnoticed. After the initial HSV infection, the virus becomes latent, traveling to the dorsal root ganglia and remaining there for the person's lifetime. Subsequent infection is caused by viral reactivation in the affected dermatome. [3]

Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642) ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.
